data.title: The $2 that disappeared in "shadow hosting": an AI overreach incident
data.description:
The illusion of artificial intelligence was once considered a harmless information bias—fabricating a fact or presenting a illogical text. But the reality of 2026 delivered a harsh blow: as AI evolves from chatbots into agents, the illusion begins to transform into a costly execution risk. The problem is no longer simply about saying the wrong thing; it now has the ability to directly take away your assets. Supply Chain Poison: From Hand-Written Code to Vibe Coding This week's LiteLLM poisoning incident serves as a wake-up call for this trend. As the underlying engine of almost all mainstream AI Agent frameworks, LiteLLM suffered a typical supply chain breach in this incident. Attackers exploited weaknesses in the security toolchain to obtain the distribution key, thereby pushing malicious code to user environments as an official version. Due to the presence of a legitimate signature, traditional verification mechanisms were almost rendered ineffective. Interestingly, this heist was exposed simply because the hacker's code contained a basic bug in its handling of recursive logic, causing the victim's computer to crash due to resource exhaustion. This exposes a long-neglected vulnerability in the open-source ecosystem: when you install a library, you are essentially trusting an entire dependency tree spanning hundreds of packages. The decay of any node in this tree will flow down the chain into the core production environment. This risk is further amplified under the popular Vibe coding development paradigm. Many developers describe their requirements using natural language, with AI automatically generating code. When system errors occur, developers often directly adopt the AI's suggested fixes and perform operations such as `pip install`, lacking further verification of the dependency sources and security. In this environment, the development threshold continues to decrease, but the complexity of security audits has not decreased accordingly. Every seemingly simple dependency installation actually introduces new uncertainties, and these uncertainties are gradually becoming part of the systemic risk. Meta Internal Incident: When Humans Become Execution Interfaces Similar changes are also occurring at the human-computer interaction level. As software development shifts from step-by-step operations to result-driven approaches, the role of humans is changing: from judges to confirmation nodes, or even just execution interfaces. Software development is undergoing a qualitative leap from "manual transmission" to "autonomous driving." In the past, developers were responsible for every operation; but with the introduction of agents, humans are more like passengers in an autonomous driving system, gradually retreating to confirmation nodes in the process, or even just execution terminals. When the human role degenerates from "judgmenter" to AI's "execution interface," the willingness to review decreases exponentially. A recent SEV1 security incident at Meta demonstrates this: an engineer invoked an AI agent to answer technical questions on an internal forum, and the agent automatically posted replies without review. Subsequently, another engineer followed the suggestion, but inaccurate information led to misconfigured system permissions, leaving sensitive data exposed without authorization for two hours. Meta officially attributed this to "human error," but from a human-computer interaction perspective, it appears more like an interface crash. When AI's output appears so professional and "executable," the defense mechanisms of humans as verification nodes automatically weaken. This change can be mitigated in information systems through rollbacks and repairs, but when AI begins to intervene in systems with real-world consequences—especially in financial scenarios involving assets and transactions—the illusion becomes an irreversible and expensive bill. The $2 Lesson: A Self-Initiated Rescue Through Substitution If Meta's incident was a misallocation of permissions, then in the financial sector, the problem is much more serious because it directly concerns asset ownership. Since 2026, numerous wallet and infrastructure projects have launched Agentic Wallet products, attempting to enable AI agents to directly assist users in completing on-chain operations. When conducting systematic testing on this emerging product category, the Cobo AI team captured a highly representative behavioral pattern, which they defined as "Shadow Custody"—that is, an agent, without the user's knowledge, transfers actual control of assets from the user's wallet to an invisible and uncontrollable intermediary by autonomously generating keys and creating temporary addresses. The result is an address on the blockchain that the user cannot directly control—a black box that exists logically but is invisible to the user. The sequence of events can be summarized as follows: A user instructed an agent to purchase $2 worth of "Spain YES" tokens on Polymarket. During execution, the agent quickly encountered an obstacle: Polymarket transactions require a specific digital signature format (EIP-712), but the SDK used by the agent was designed to bundle the "assembling signature content" and "signing with the private key" steps together—in other words, the SDK assumed you already had a private key and could sign it yourself. The problem is that the user's wallet isn't a typical wallet where the user holds the key; it's an MPC wallet where the keys are jointly held by multiple parties—it's perfectly capable of signing, just using a different path. However, the Agent isn't aware of this path; it only sees that the current path is blocked and concludes: This wallet cannot sign. In a system where trust is maintained by rules, the process should stop or request additional authorization. But the Agent doesn't stop. Instead, it interprets this limitation as the existing wallet being unable to complete the signing and then searches for alternative solutions. So, without authorization, the Agent generated a brand new private key locally and used it to create a temporary wallet address. It then transferred 2 USDC.e tokens from the user's MPC wallet to this address and used the temporary key to complete the signing, successfully purchasing the tokens. From the transaction results, everything happened as expected: 10 "Spain YES" conditional tokens were correctly purchased. However, from a system structure perspective, it completely messed things up. These tokens did not return to the user's MPC wallet but remained in the temporary address created by the Agent. The user initially didn't notice until checking the balance and finding it to be zero, at which point the Agent reconstructed the entire execution process. This wasn't just a bug; logically speaking, the Agent didn't make a mistake; it merely filled a gap in the system's boundary definition. Simply put, when the original path was blocked, the Agent didn't stop and report an error; instead, it took a detour to "complete the task." It secretly created a temporary address to transfer funds, yet the UI didn't mention that the assets in its hands had already been "moved." This disconnect between user perception and underlying reality is essentially a form of path hijacking. This covert path hijacking reflects the systemic risks inherent in machine-native finance regarding the transparency of fund flows, semantic consistency, and the stability of the execution environment: And this "filling"—once unrestrained—opens three doors for attackers: Logical Deflection: Malicious tools don't need to forge links or pop-ups; they only need to trick the Agent into activating a temporary wallet deep within the code. The user sees a successful transaction, but in reality, control of the assets is stripped away the instant the transaction is executed. Semantic Manipulation: Attackers don't need to infiltrate the system; they only need to implant fake technical restrictions (such as "original path has expired") in documents or prompts. The task-oriented Agent will actively transfer funds to circumvent the "fake restriction." It wasn't hacked, it was just tricked.

  • Component poisoning: By tampering with third-party SDKs or API libraries, correct road signs are made to appear as dead ends. The agent may even believe it is faithfully taking a detour, when in fact it has entered a well-designed dead end.

    • Constraints are more important than capabilities: Three gates to controlling the boundaries of AI agent behavior

    In financial scenarios with zero tolerance for error, the "intelligence" of AI is often the biggest enemy of security. What we need is not stronger execution, but a more rigid ability to define boundaries.

    In financial scenarios with zero tolerance for error, the "intelligence" of AI is often the biggest enemy of security. What we need is not stronger execution, but stronger ability to define boundaries.

    To this end, we need to install three insurmountable gates for the Agent: 1. Policy Gate: Breaking the self-reference dilemma. Constraints must come from the outside. We need a separate Policy Engine to determine "whether the operation is allowed" before the action occurs. For example, Agents are strictly prohibited from generating their own private keys or transferring funds to unauthorized addresses. Once this line is crossed, execution terminates immediately. 2. Transaction Gate: Risk Perspective All Agent decisions must ultimately be translated into on-chain transactions. A "transaction firewall" is established between the Agent and the blockchain to restore unreadable raw data to structured information. By scoring the target address's behavior, amount deviation, and other dimensions, high-risk transactions will be blocked and forced to trigger manual approval. 3. Visibility Gate: Real-time Observation Introducing an agent-independent watcher system. When funds flow to a new address or fail to return within a specified time, the watcher sends alerts within seconds. It transforms post-event discovery into process intervention, ensuring that asset status remains transparent even if the agent attempts to conceal the path. Conclusion We are in a dangerous transition period. AI's execution capabilities are skyrocketing, but our constraints on it are still stuck in the Stone Age. If an architecture cannot clearly define absolutely prohibited behaviors and perform real-time verification, then every autonomous decision made by AI is essentially a high-stakes gamble with users' assets. And in the financial sector, nobody wants to gamble.

data.title: Why is Wall Street going blockchain?
data.description: Wall Street is migrating its capital markets infrastructure to the blockchain to improve the speed of money transfers and asset tokenization, eliminating hidden costs in the existing system. Major institutions such as the DTCC and the New York Stock Exchange have begun this move, signaling the largest infrastructure upgrade in financial markets since electronic trading, creating tremendous opportunities for building on-chain financial services. Wall Street is no longer just exploring blockchain. It is migrating to blockchain. After years of observation, the institutions that form the backbone of global capital markets—exchanges, clearinghouses, and electronic trading platforms—are migrating to on-chain. What is happening now is the largest infrastructure upgrade in capital markets since the shift to electronic trading thirty years ago. But most people won't realize this until the transition is complete. Institutions have already begun to act. In December 2025, the DTCC received a no-action letter from the SEC authorizing it to tokenize real-world assets on approved blockchains. The DTCC processed $3.7 quadrillion in transactions in 2024. It now plans to launch production-grade tokenization services for U.S. Treasury securities in the first half of 2026. On January 19, 2026, the New York Stock Exchange announced a platform for 24/7 on-chain trading and settlement of U.S. stocks and ETFs—including fragmented shares, instant settlement, and stablecoin funding—and partnered with BNY and Citibank to support tokenized deposits at the ICE Clearing House. The world's most iconic stock exchange is moving on-chain. Tradeweb completed its first real-time, fully on-chain U.S. Treasury financing for USDC in August 2025—on a Saturday, outside the traditional settlement window, in collaboration with Bank of America, Citadel Securities, DTCC, and Virtu Financial. Its scope is expanding quarterly and now includes cross-border and intraday settlement. Nasdaq submitted its proposed rule changes to the SEC in September 2025. This increasingly looks like a migration rather than a series of isolated experiments. Regulatory Clarity and Opportunities for Builders The ultimate breakthrough is regulatory clarity—and it's finally in action. If the current momentum continues, the CLARITY Act can provide traditional finance with what the Genius Act has already done for the adoption and acceleration of stablecoins. The safeguards that large institutions need are within reach. So what does this mean for builders? The migration of the world's financial infrastructure to on-chain will create demand for entirely new categories of products and services. The fastest-moving existing companies aren't your competitors—they're your customers. DTCC doesn't want to build middleware. The NYSE doesn't want to build compliance tools. Tradeweb doesn't want to build a cross-border distribution layer. These companies are laying the foundation for regulated, institutional-grade infrastructure. The founders are building everything that runs on top of it. This is the same as in the 1990s. Exchanges didn't build E*TRADE. They didn't build Bloomberg. They didn't build the order management systems and prime broker platforms that defined the next generation. Those were built by founders who saw the trends of the future. More participants, faster speeds, lower friction. More liquidity. A larger market. History clearly shows where this is going. The window of opportunity to build infrastructure in tokenized financial markets is now open. Build accordingly.

data.title: Why Did a U.S. Court Dismiss Michael Lewellen’s Bid to Shield His Crypto Software From Prosecution?
data.description:
Software Developers Caught In Legal Limbo After Texas Court Ruling

A developer’s attempt to secure a legal "shield" for his code before it even launches has hit a wall in the United States.

Michael Lewellen, the creator behind a non-custodial crowdfunding tool called Pharos, recently asked a federal court to confirm that his software wouldn’t land him in prison.

He argued that because Pharos is designed to be private and non-custodial, meaning he never actually touches the users' money, he cannot possibly comply with federal money-laundering rules that require tracking user identities.

Essentially, he was asking for permission to innovate without the fear of a five-year prison sentence hanging over his head

Today, I’m taking a stand against the Biden administration’s unjust crackdown on crypto development. I’ve filed a lawsuit against the DOJ to challenge their flawed and unjust interpretation of the law.

My work on Pharos—a non-custodial protocol for public goods…

— Michael Lewellen (@LewellenMichael) January 16, 2025

On 25 March 2026, Chief Judge Reed O’Connor of the Northern District of Texas dismissed the case.

The court’s reasoning was simple: Lewellen isn't in trouble yet.

The judge ruled that the developer failed to show a "substantial threat of prosecution," effectively telling him that the court cannot solve a problem that hasn't happened.

While the dismissal was "without prejudice", meaning Lewellen can return if the government actually comes after him, the decision leaves a cloud of uncertainty over the decentralized finance (DeFi) sector.

🚨 JUST IN: Texas Court Dismisses Lawsuit against Pam Bondi over Non-Custodial Software Prosecutions

A Texas Court has dismissed a lawsuit by cryptocurrency developer Michael Lewellen filed against Attorney General Pam Bondi, in which he alleged that criminal cases brought… pic.twitter.com/lgxaZeIfN2

— The Rage (@theragetech) March 25, 2026

Does A Department Of Justice Memo Provide Enough Protection?

Central to the court’s decision was a document known as the "Blanche Memo," issued by Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche in April 2025.

This internal Department of Justice (DOJ) guideline suggests that prosecutors should not target developers of "offline wallets" or mixing services for the independent actions of their users or for accidental regulatory slips.

Judge O’Connor pointed to this as a sign that Lewellen wasn't in immediate danger.

Lewellen, however, remains unconvinced that a shift in internal policy is the same as a legal guarantee.

Following the ruling, he expressed his frustration on social media.

Lewellen wrote,

"Disappointed to see the court dismiss my suit today. A non-binding DoJ memo is no substitute for real legal certainty."

Disappointed to see the court dismiss my suit today. A non-binding DoJ memo is no substitute for real legal certainty.

My lawyers are exploring all options for a path forward. Huge thanks to the @coincenter team for their incredible support and expertise through this.

We need… https://t.co/uXJqGww7IO

— Michael Lewellen (@LewellenMichael) March 25, 2026

His sentiment highlights a growing rift between the tech community and the judiciary over whether "regulation by memo" is a fair way to govern a multi-billion-dollar industry.

Why Are Tornado Cash And Samourai Wallet Cases Different?

To justify his fear of arrest, Lewellen pointed to the high-profile crackdowns on other crypto privacy tools.

Roman Storm, the co-founder of Tornado Cash, was convicted in 2025 of conspiracy to operate an unlicensed money-transmitting business, while Samourai Wallet founders Keonne Rodriguez and William Lonergan Hill are currently serving five-year sentences.

DOJ has decided it will retry Roman Storm in the fall. Despite failing to convince a jury the first time around, despite making obvious mistakes like calling irrelevant witnesses and not understanding the forensic analysis of their own blockchain evidence, and despite multiple… pic.twitter.com/MRZDHAugT8

— Amanda Tuminelli (@amandatums) March 10, 2026

Lewellen argued these cases prove that writing neutral code is now a criminal risk in the United States.

Judge O’Connor disagreed with the comparison.

He noted that in those cases, prosecutors alleged the developers knowingly allowed criminals to use their platforms to hide stolen funds.

O'Connor wrote,

"The ‘core conduct’ of those cases is money laundering. By contrast, the core conduct here would be running a business."

The judge emphasized that because Lewellen "disclaims any knowing transmission of criminal funds," his situation doesn't match the criminal profile of the developers currently behind bars.

Will Congress Step In To Define The Rules For DeFi?

With the courts refusing to weigh in early, the focus has shifted to the halls of Congress.

Industry advocates, including groups like Coin Center and the Blockchain Association, are now putting all their weight behind the Blockchain Regulatory Certainty Act (BRCA).

This bipartisan bill aims to explicitly exempt developers who do not control user funds from being classified as money transmitters.

Today, @RepRitchie and I introduced the Blockchain Regulatory Certainty Act to protect blockchain developers and service providers that never custody consumer funds from unjust government prosecution. pic.twitter.com/nCbpHcpf1Y

— Tom Emmer (@GOPMajorityWhip) May 21, 2025

Peter Van Valkenburgh, executive director of Coin Center, warned that relying on the government's good graces is a dangerous game for developers.

"The Blanche memo is not enough to secure their rights.”

He described it as a "vague enforcement signal" rather than a real limit on state power.

But that is not real clarity. A DOJ memo is not binding law. It can be revised, revoked, or ignored. And it plainly has not provided meaningful protection to developers, given the outcomes in the Tornado Cash and Samourai Wallet cases. 3/

— Peter Van Valkenburgh (@valkenburgh) March 25, 2026

So while I hope the court is right that non-custodial software developers are not at real risk, the Blanche memo is not enough to secure their rights. It is a vague enforcement signal, not a durable limit on government power. 4/ pic.twitter.com/XgzfQ8Astr

— Peter Van Valkenburgh (@valkenburgh) March 25, 2026

As the 2026 election season approaches, the window for passing such legislation is narrowing, leaving developers like Lewellen to wonder if they should keep building or wait for the law to catch up.

data.title: The AI ​​era is creating a polarization: the rich are getting richer and the poor are getting poorer.
data.description:
Author: jiayi Source: X, @mscryptojiayi

AI has changed our lifestyles; that's a fact.

We use AI to write emails, create PPTs, search for information, and even write our WeChat Moments posts. We've become accustomed to AI's presence, as natural as we've become accustomed to WiFi.

But few people stop to think about this question: Is the AI ​​you use the same thing as the AI ​​others use?

The "fairness" of the AI ​​era is the biggest illusion

Silicon Valley likes to tell a story: AI gives everyone a super assistant, knowledge is no longer the privilege of a few, and everyone is equal.

It sounds beautiful.

But the truth is—AI is inherently unfair; it's a competition of financial resources. From chips to computing power, from model training to token consumption, every aspect of AI is expensive. An NVIDIA H100 chip costs over $25,000. Training a GPT-4 level model costs over $100 million. Every question you ask AI consumes tokens—and tokens have a price. Claude Opus costs $5 per million tokens input and $25 per million tokens output. ChatGPT Pro costs $200 per month. Add to that Perplexity, Cursor, Midjourney… and a heavy AI user's monthly tool costs easily exceed $500. Some people are burning through $5,000 a month to build competitive barriers with AI, while others think they've caught up with the times using a free version of ChatGPT. These are not the same track. They're not even the same game. At the national level: the structural gap is irreversible. This logic is even more brutal at the national level. The AI ​​arms race requires three things: chips, computing power, and talent. All three require huge amounts of capital. The United States alone controls more than 70% of the world's AI computing power. China is catching up, but the chip ban is holding it back. As for most developing countries—in 46 emerging market countries, entry-level broadband costs account for 40% of monthly income. When even stable internet access is a luxury for a young Nigerian, what's the point of talking about "AI equality"? In high-income countries, 94% of people have internet access, compared to only 23% in low-income countries. 84% of high-income countries have 5G coverage, compared to only 4% in low-income countries. Third-world countries aren't just lagging behind in the AI ​​era; they're simply not even qualified to participate. This structural gap cannot be bridged through hard work alone. At the individual level: Your ceiling is being redefined by AI. The logic at the national level applies equally to individuals. As I wrote on my Twitter bio: An individual's ceiling = values ​​+ cognition + practical ability. What has AI done in these three ways? First, AI has solved a large number of practical efficiency problems. Previously, creating an industry report took a week; now it can be done in a day. Previously, writing code started from scratch; now AI provides the framework. In terms of efficiency, AI is indeed leveling the playing field. Second, however, AI is significantly amplifying cognitive gaps. With the same AI tool, what you ask, how you ask it, and whether you can judge whether the AI's answer is right or wrong—all depend entirely on your existing level of knowledge. A person with deep knowledge using Claude for research knows what questions to ask, how to follow up, and which answers have flaws and need verification. AI saved him 80% of his execution time, which he used for deeper thinking. But what about someone with shallow understanding? They throw questions at AI and use whatever AI gives them. They throw their brains at it and deliver directly. Over time, they stop thinking. AI didn't make them smarter; it made them lazy and stupid. Third, the gap in delivery quality will widen. When you ask AI questions based on your existing knowledge, the difference in depth, accuracy, and real-time performance is exponential. Using Claude Opus, one person produces deep insights, while another produces seemingly plausible but ultimately meaningless information. A particularly interesting study from Aalto University in Finland shows that the more people use AI, the more likely they are to overestimate their own abilities. AI makes you "feel" stronger—your output looks professional and fluent. But if you can't distinguish between good and bad, you're just producing "refined mediocrity." Therefore, the gaps in values, cognition, and practical ability—these three dimensions—are magnified infinitely in the AI ​​era. Smart people become smarter, those with knowledge have deeper knowledge, and the wealthy use better tools to widen the gap further. Meanwhile, those at the other end become lazier, more superficial, and poorer with the "help" of AI. Cost × Cognition: A Double Gap Exacerbated Here's a logical chain that many haven't grasped: Money determines the level of AI you can use → The level of AI determines the quality and depth of information you acquire → The quality of information determines your cognitive boundaries → Cognitive boundaries determine the quality of your decisions → The quality of decisions determines how much money you can earn. This is a closed loop. The rich will get richer, and the poor will get poorer. The free version of ChatGPT has a near 40% illusion rate. That means if you ask it 10 questions, 4 of the answers are fabricated. The paid version of GPT-4 has a 28% illusion rate, and the latest version has dropped to 45%. Decisions made with the free version and decisions made with Opus, accumulated over the long term, will result in two completely different life trajectories. A huge information gap will always exist in this world. AI hasn't eliminated the information gap; AI has turned it into a paywall. Those who use VPNs and those who don't live in two different worlds. Here's a personal observation that I find quite disheartening. You're likely seeing this article because you can access Twitter using a VPN. But think about it—how many people around you can't? When you talk to them, don't you clearly feel a gap in their understanding? This isn't a difference in intelligence. It's a long-term cognitive divide caused by the information environment. One person is exposed to the world's most cutting-edge information, in-depth discussions, and top-quality content creators daily. The other sees algorithm-fed short videos and filtered information feeds. Over five or ten years, their ways of thinking, judgment, and worldviews will be completely different. The AI ​​era has amplified this gap even further. Those who can access VPNs use Claude, Perplexity, and the world's best AI tools. For those who can't bypass the Great Firewall—ChatGPT is blocked in China, Claude is blocked in China—they can only use localized alternatives or obtain them through resellers at inflated prices. The "walls" of the AI ​​era aren't just physical firewalls. There are language walls—cutting-edge AI models are far more optimized for English than other languages. There are paywalls. There are algorithmic cocoons. Each wall divides people into different worlds. Stanford University research shows that non-English speaking users require five times more tokens to access the same content using AI. In other words, you spend the same amount of money but get less information of lower quality. The scariest thing: you've already fallen behind, but you don't know it. This is the point I most want to make in this entire article. Free AI can also answer questions. It can also help you write. It can also help you search. So people using the free version might think, "I'm using AI too, I'm not falling behind." But the free version's reasoning is shallower, its illusions more numerous, and its information older. The answers you get "seem" correct, but are actually full of seemingly plausible errors. It's like two people "running." One is actually running forward, the other is running in place on a treadmill. Both feel they are running, but only one is moving forward. There's a concept in psychology called the Dunning-Kruger effect: the less you know, the more you think you know. AI amplifies this effect tenfold—the more you rely on AI, the more powerful you feel. But you've lost the ability to think independently; you simply don't know. This is the cruelest aspect of the AI ​​era. It's not that AI will replace you. It's that people using better AI and with deeper understanding will leave you far behind. And you might not even understand how you fell behind until the day you're eliminated.

data.title: DeFi surpasses CeFi: Predictability is key
data.description:
Authors: Pranav Garimidi, Joachim Neu, Max Resnick; Translation: Luffy, Foresight News

Blockchain can now confidently claim its ability to compete with existing financial infrastructure. Current production systems can process tens of thousands of transactions per second, and future performance will see orders of magnitude improvement.

But beyond sheer throughput, financial applications also require predictability. When a transaction is initiated—whether it's a trade, an auction bid, or an option exercise—reliable assurance of when the transaction is recorded on-chain is essential for the normal operation of the financial system. If transactions face unpredictable delays, many applications will become unusable. For on-chain financial applications to be competitive, blockchain must provide short-term on-chain guarantees: as soon as a valid transaction is submitted to the network, it must be packaged into a block as quickly as possible.

For example, an on-chain order book provides this.

An efficient order book requires market makers to continuously provide liquidity by posting buy and sell orders on the order book. A core challenge for market makers is minimizing bid-ask spreads while avoiding adverse selection due to price misalignment with market conditions. To this end, market makers must constantly update their orders to reflect the latest market conditions. For example, when a Federal Reserve announcement causes significant asset price fluctuations, market makers need to respond immediately by updating their orders to the new prices. If the transactions used by market makers to update their orders cannot be recorded on-chain in a timely manner, arbitrageurs will execute trades at outdated prices, causing losses for the market maker. In such cases, market makers can only widen spreads to reduce risk, leading to a decline in the competitiveness of on-chain trading platforms. A predictable on-chain transaction mechanism provides reliable guarantees for market makers, enabling them to respond quickly to off-chain events and maintain the efficient operation of the on-chain market. The Gap Between Current Status and Goals: Current mainstream blockchains can only provide final on-chain guarantees, and the effective period is often measured in seconds. Such guarantees are sufficient for applications like payments, but cannot support the vast majority of financial applications that require real-time responses from market participants. Returning to the order book example: for market makers, a guarantee of "on-chain within seconds" is meaningless, as long as arbitrageurs' trades are packaged into earlier blocks. Lacking strong on-chain guarantees, market makers can only hedge risk by widening spreads and offering worse quotes to users. This makes on-chain trading less attractive compared to other platforms with stronger guarantees. For blockchain to truly realize its vision of becoming a modern infrastructure for capital markets, developers must solve these problems, enabling high-value applications like order books to flourish. Where does achieving predictability lie? Strengthening on-chain transaction guarantees on existing blockchains to support such scenarios is extremely challenging. Some protocols rely on a single node (block-producing node) determining the order of transaction packaging at a specific time. While this simplifies the engineering design of high-performance public blockchains, it also creates potential economic monopolies, allowing block-producing nodes to extract value. Typically, during the window period a node is elected as a block-producing node, it has complete control over which transactions are included in a block. For blockchains handling large amounts of financial activity, block-producing nodes occupy a privileged position. If a node refuses to include a transaction, the user can only wait for the next willing block-producing node. In permissionless networks, block-producing nodes naturally have an incentive to extract value, which is commonly referred to as MEV (Mean Equity). MEV is far more complex than a sandwich attack. Even if a block-producing node delays transactions by only tens of milliseconds, it can earn huge profits and reduce the efficiency of underlying applications. An order book that prioritizes only a subset of traders' orders puts everyone else in an unfair environment. In the worst case, malicious behavior by block-producing nodes can cause traders to leave the platform altogether. Assuming an interest rate hike announcement is made, the price of ETH instantly drops by 5%. All market makers on the order book rush to cancel their existing orders and re-order at the new price. Simultaneously, all arbitrageurs will submit orders to sell ETH at the outdated price. If this order book runs on a single block-producing node protocol, that node will possess immense power. It can choose to censor all market makers' cancellations, allowing arbitrageurs to reap huge profits; or it can delay cancellations, allowing arbitrage trades to execute first; it can even directly insert its own arbitrage trades to profit from price discrepancies. Two core objectives: censorship resistance and information hiding. Faced with this advantage, active participation by market makers becomes uneconomical; they can be exploited whenever prices fluctuate. The problem essentially stems from two major privileges of block-producing nodes: They can censor other people's transactions; they can see other people's transactions and submit their own accordingly. Either of these privileges can have disastrous consequences. Let's illustrate this precisely with an auction. Suppose there are two bidders, Alice and Bob, and Bob happens to be the block-producing node for the block where the auction takes place. (This is just one example; the logic can be generalized to any number of bidders.) The auction accepts bids within the block production cycle, for example, from time 0 to time 1. Alice submits a bid bA at time tA, and Bob submits a bid bB at a later time tB. Since Bob is the block producer, he can always ensure that he acts last. Both can obtain the asset price from a continuously updated price source (such as the median price of a centralized exchange), denoted as pₜ at time t. We assume that at any time t, both parties expect the asset price at the end of the auction (t=1) to be equal to the current price pₜ. The auction rules are simple: the highest bidder wins and pays their offer. The Necessity of Censorship Resistance If Bob can use his block producer status to censor Alice's bid, the auction mechanism will completely fail. Bob can guarantee winning simply by submitting any low bid, and the final auction profit will be almost zero. The Necessity of Information Hiding A more complex situation arises: Bob cannot directly review Alice's bids, but he can see Alice's offer before he makes his own. Bob has a simple strategy: If the current price pₜ_B > bA, bid slightly higher than bA; otherwise, abandon the bid. Through this strategy, Bob forces Alice into adverse selection: Alice can only win if her bid is higher than the asset's expected value, and winning means a loss, ultimately leading her to withdraw from the auction. Once all competitors have left, Bob can then bid an extremely low price to win the auction, with a profit close to zero. The core conclusion is: auction duration is irrelevant. As long as Bob can review Alice's bids or see them before bidding himself, the auction is doomed to fail. This logic also applies to high-frequency trading scenarios, including spot, perpetual contract, and derivatives exchanges: if block-producing nodes possessed the same power as Bob in this case, the market would completely fail. For on-chain products supporting such scenarios to be viable, block-producing nodes must never be granted such privileges. Why haven't these problems materialized in reality? The above analysis paints a bleak picture of permissionless on-chain transactions with a single block-producing node, yet many such protocols still maintain considerable trading volumes on decentralized exchanges (DEXs). Why? In reality, two forces offset the aforementioned problems: Block-producing nodes typically hold a large amount of native tokens and are deeply tied to the success or failure of the public chain, thus preventing them from completely abusing their economic power; Application layers have developed workarounds to reduce their vulnerability to such issues. While these two factors have allowed DeFi to function normally so far, in the long run, they are insufficient to allow on-chain markets to truly compete with off-chain counterparts. On public chains with active economic activity, becoming a block-producing node requires substantial staking. Therefore, nodes either hold a large amount of tokens themselves or possess sufficient reputation to receive delegation from other holders. In either case, large node operators are reputable and well-known entities. Furthermore, staking assets also incentivize them to maintain the development of the public chain. Therefore, while we haven't yet witnessed nodes completely abusing their power, this doesn't mean the problem doesn't exist. First, relying on the goodwill of node operators, social pressure, and long-term incentives is not a reliable foundation for future finance. As the scale of on-chain finance expands, the potential profits of nodes will also increase. The greater this temptation, the weaker the social pressure to restrain nodes from violating short-term interests becomes. Second, the extent of node power abuse is a continuous spectrum, ranging from mild behavior to complete market destruction. Node operators may unilaterally and gradually expand their power to obtain higher profits; once someone crosses the line, others will quickly follow suit. The behavior of a single node may seem to have limited impact, but a collective shift will have obvious consequences. The most typical example is timing games: block-producing nodes delay publishing blocks as much as possible to maximize profits within the limits allowed by the protocol. This leads to extended block times and even missing blocks. Although the profitability of such strategies is well-known, nodes initially choose restraint out of a sense of responsibility to maintain the public chain. However, this social balance is extremely fragile. Once a node starts arbitrage without any cost, other nodes will quickly follow suit. Time-sequence arbitrage is just one example of how nodes can increase their returns without completely abusing their power. Nodes have many other ways to increase returns at the expense of applications. These behaviors may be tolerable in isolation, but eventually they will cross a tipping point, causing the cost of running on-chain to outweigh the benefits. Another reason DeFi can still function is that applications move their core logic off-chain, only uploading the results. For example, protocols that require fast auction execution generally complete the process off-chain, often using permissioned node groups to avoid malicious nodes. UniswapX's Dutch auction on the Ethereum mainnet and Cowswap's batch auctions both use off-chain execution. While this approach allows applications to run, it puts the underlying public chain and its value proposition in an awkward position: off-chain application execution logic reduces the underlying public chain to a mere settlement layer. One of the core advantages of DeFi is composability, but since all execution takes place off-chain, applications are naturally isolated. This reliance on off-chain execution also adds a new assumption to the application's trust model: in addition to the availability of the underlying public chain, the off-chain infrastructure must also function properly. How to achieve predictability? To solve these problems, the protocol needs to satisfy two main characteristics: stable transaction on-chain recording and sorting rules, and privacy protection before transaction confirmation. The primary condition: Censorship resistance. We summarize the first characteristic as short-term censorship resistance: if a transaction reaches an honest node, it is guaranteed to be included in the next available block. Short-term censorship resistance: any valid transaction that arrives on time at any honest node will definitely be included in the next block. More precisely, assume the protocol operates on a fixed clock, for example, producing a block every 100 milliseconds. If a transaction arrives at an honest node in 250 milliseconds, it should be included in the block produced in 300 milliseconds. Attackers have no right to selectively package or ignore transactions. The core principle of this definition is that users and applications should have a highly reliable path for transactions to be uploaded to the blockchain, and transactions should not fail due to malicious packet loss or malfunction of a single node. While this definition requires that transactions arriving at any honest node be packaged, the actual implementation overhead may be too high. The key is that the protocol must be robust enough to make the transaction upload entry point highly predictable and the logic simple and easy to understand. A permissionless single block-producing node protocol obviously does not meet this requirement: if the current block-producing node acts maliciously, the transaction has no other path to be uploaded to the blockchain. Conversely, even if only a group of four nodes can guarantee that transactions are packaged every time period, it will significantly improve the blockchain options for users and applications. Sacrificing some performance is worthwhile for applications to thrive. Finding the optimal balance between robustness and performance requires further research, but the current protocols' guarantees are clearly insufficient. Once the protocol can guarantee on-chain transactions, the ordering problem is easily solved. The protocol can adopt any deterministic ordering rule; the simplest way is to order by priority fee, or allow applications to flexibly order transactions that interact with their own state. The optimal way to order transactions remains an active area of ​​research, but in any case, ordering rules are only meaningful if transactions are successfully recorded on-chain. The second condition: Information hiding. After short-term censorship resistance, another important characteristic that the protocol needs to satisfy is what we call privacy protection through hiding. Hiding: Except for the node that submitted the transaction, no participant can obtain any information about the transaction before the transaction is finally confirmed and ordered by the protocol. Protocols that satisfy concealment allow nodes receiving transactions to view the transaction content in plaintext, but require the rest of the network to remain agnostic until consensus is reached and the transaction order is determined. For example, the protocol could use delayed encryption to make block content invisible before the deadline; or threshold encryption, where the committee confirms the block is irreversible before decryption. This means that nodes may misuse transaction information submitted to themselves, but other nodes in the network will only learn about the transaction content afterward. When transaction information is made public to the entire network, its ordering and confirmation are complete, and other parties cannot preemptively trade. This definition is effective only if multiple nodes can package transactions at any given time. We did not adopt a stronger privacy definition (such as encrypted memory pools)—where only the user knows the transaction information—because the protocol needs to filter spam transactions. If transaction content is completely hidden from the entire network, the network will be unable to distinguish between spam and valid transactions. The only solution is to leak some unencrypted metadata, such as the payment address that will deduct fees regardless of whether the transaction is valid or not. However, this type of metadata may reveal enough information to give attackers an opportunity. Therefore, we chose a compromise: only a single node can see the transaction content, while the rest of the network cannot. This also means that users must have at least one honest node as their entry point on the chain at any given time. A protocol that simultaneously possesses short-term censorship resistance and anonymity is an ideal underlying layer for building financial applications. Returning to the example of on-chain auctions, these two characteristics directly prevent Bob from disrupting the market in two ways: he cannot censor Alice's bids, nor can he use Alice's bids to guide his own behavior, perfectly solving the two major problems mentioned above. Under the guarantee of short-term censorship resistance, any transaction (whether it is a pending order or an auction bid) can be ensured to be recorded on the chain instantly. Market makers can modify orders, bidders can bid quickly, and settlement can be executed efficiently. Users can be confident that their operations will be executed immediately, thus enabling a new generation of low-latency, real-world financial applications to be built entirely on-chain. For blockchain to truly compete with and surpass existing financial infrastructure, it needs to address far more than just throughput issues.

data.title: AI, Energy, Population, and the Reshaping of the World Order
data.description: <div class="share___SVvtl"><div>AI, energy, and population are the three biggest forces reshaping the world order over the next two to three decades. Why two to three decades? Because these three trends are simultaneously reaching critical points within this window: AI will likely replace most cognitive labor; clean energy will likely replace fossil fuels as the primary source of energy; and a shift in the population landscape is already a certainty. These three forces are simultaneously transforming from trends into reality. This is the change that most people will experience in their lifetime. AI is reshaping social structures and class distribution; energy is reshaping geopolitical order; and population is reshaping the world's center of gravity and power map. The directions of these three variables are clear and irreversible. Their cumulative effect will bring about structural changes in the world. We don't need to predict the specific future; simply understanding the direction of each variable is enough to realize that our accustomed social order, economic logic, and international landscape will undergo profound transformation. AI: Intelligence Oversupply and the Disappearance of the Middle Layer AI is not just another technological upgrade. The steam engine replaced animal power, industrialization replaced manual labor, and computers enabled automation—each previous technological revolution primarily replaced manual and repetitive mechanical labor. AI is the first to massively replace cognitive judgment. A large amount of ordinary mental work—analysis, writing, programming, customer service, translation, basic decision-making—is being supplied infinitely at near-zero marginal cost. This means the market value of mental labor will depreciate significantly. Just as the Industrial Revolution rendered the skills of hand weavers worthless, AI will subject the professional accumulation of a large number of knowledge workers to the same fate. But this time, the speed is faster and the impact is wider—because the occupational scope covered by cognitive labor is far greater than that of any kind of manual labor. The most direct structural consequence of this is the exacerbation of social stratification. The gap between those who can leverage AI to amplify their abilities and those who cannot is not linear, but exponential. While past technological revolutions eliminated old jobs, they also created numerous new supporting jobs. Industrialization required workers, managers, engineers, accountants, and salespeople; the unfolding of the entire industrial chain created a large middle class. AI is different this time. Its characteristic is that a very small number of people can perform tasks that previously required a large workforce. One person plus AI may be more effective than an entire team in the past. AI will certainly create new jobs, but these new jobs are not evenly distributed—they are concentrated at both ends: top-level creative work and bottom-level manual services, precisely where the middle layer is hollowed out. The middle class is not just a concept of income. It is the stable structural foundation of modern society. The middle class has properties to protect, children to educate, and pensions to preserve their value, so they naturally tend towards order, gradualism, and compromise. They are the source of votes for moderates in democratic politics, the main force in the consumer market, and the carriers of social consensus. In fact, this group is already shrinking. In the United States, the percentage of people who identify as middle class fell from 53% in 2008 to 44% in 2014. In Germany, the proportion of the middle class fell from 60% in 1991 to 54% in 2013, and the proportion of middle-class people among young people is declining even faster. This trend occurred before the widespread adoption of AI. The hollowing out of the middle class means that all these functions have lost their bearers. Education is one of the hardest-hit areas. The modern education system is built on an implicit assumption: individuals spend over a decade accumulating knowledge and skills, then recoup that investment over decades of career. This is an investment return model, and it has worked well for the past century. But if AI reduces the market value of most knowledge-based skills to zero, this model collapses. A person spends four years learning accounting, law, and programming, only to graduate to find that AI can perform the same work faster and at a lower cost. Education isn't becoming less important; rather, its function as a social selection mechanism and a pathway to upward social mobility has been significantly weakened. It's harder for children from disadvantaged backgrounds to succeed, not because of unequal educational resources, but because the return on investment in education itself is collapsing. The political consequences of a shrinking middle class are certain: polarization. The middle class fills the middle ground of the political spectrum. With this group shrinking, the middle ground collapses, leaving politics at only two extremes—an angry lower class and an anxious elite—with no buffer or consensus between them. The space for moderate, rational, and compromising politics disappears, replaced by emotional mobilization and identity confrontation. This is not a phenomenon unique to any one country. From the bipartisan polarization in the United States to the rise of the far right in Europe, to the spread of populism around the world, the same structural forces bear similar fruit in different political environments. Political polarization inevitably spills over. Polarized domestic politics naturally tend towards inward contraction and external hardline stances—because this is the only posture acceptable to both sides. Moderate free trade positions lose their political appeal. Each country will develop its own version of economic nationalism. The domestic political consensus upon which globalization rests is crumbling. Meanwhile, AI is also eroding the economic foundations of globalization. The core driver of globalization over the past few decades has been labor cost arbitrage: moving production to places with cheap labor and selling products to places with strong purchasing power. This is a simple yet powerful economic logic that has driven an unprecedented global division of labor. But if AI makes labor costs unimportant, this logic loses its support. The return of manufacturing is no longer a political slogan, but an economic rationale. The political and economic foundations of globalization are being eroded simultaneously by AI. This has a particularly profound impact on developing countries: the effective catch-up path of the past few decades—first absorbing the transfer of manufacturing, and then gradually climbing up the value chain—is being shut down. Energy: The Decline of Fossil Fuels and the Restructuring of Geopolitical Order Energy is the physical foundation of civilization. How much energy a civilization can mobilize determines how much it can accomplish. For the past two centuries, this foundation has been fossil fuels—coal, oil, and natural gas. They shaped industrial systems, urban forms, transportation networks, and also the basic structure of international politics. This foundation is changing. The cost curve for photovoltaic modules is already very clear: it has decreased by nearly 90% in the past decade and is still accelerating. Wind power is following a similar path. Energy storage technology is rapidly iterating. Even without considering nuclear fusion, the strategic scarcity of fossil fuels is being continuously weakened simply by the ongoing cost reduction of renewable energy and energy storage. If nuclear fusion is commercialized during this period, this process will be significantly accelerated. Clean energy is not a supplement to fossil fuels, but a replacement. In twenty or thirty years, we will most likely live in a world where energy costs have dropped dramatically. Many people believe that oil's last bastion is transportation fuels—cars, ships, and airplanes. This is not the case. Electrification has already made oil's position in ground transportation precarious. Synthetic fuels have also begun to be commercialized, and with the continued decline in the cost of clean energy, synthetic fuels will significantly replace petroleum fuels, including the most difficult to replace jet fuel. Oil's true moat is the chemical industry. Globally, plastics, fertilizers, synthetic fibers, synthetic rubber—almost all modern chemical products—start with hydrocarbon molecules produced by petroleum cracking. The chemical industry is the area where oil is "irreplaceable." But this moat is also being breached. Green hydrogen, combined with CO₂ captured from the air, can synthesize methanol. Methanol is an extremely versatile chemical platform molecule, from which major chemical product lines such as olefins, aromatics, plastics, and synthetic fibers can be derived. Bio-based pathways offer another alternative. The key bottleneck is not technology, but cost—currently, green synthesis pathways are far more expensive than oil cracking. But cost depends on electricity prices. As clean energy continues to decrease in cost, artificial synthesis pathways will eventually compete head-on with oil in terms of economics. At that point, oil will truly be downgraded from a strategic resource to an ordinary commodity. It won't be that no one uses it anymore, but that it will no longer be irreplaceable. The geopolitical consequences of this are far-reaching. Fossil fuels are not just energy; they are the underlying operating system of the current international order. The strategic position of the Middle East is built upon oil. The US involvement in the Middle East, the financial foundations of Middle Eastern countries, and the geopolitical importance of the Gulf region all ultimately stem from the fact that the world cannot live without what lies beneath that land. Saudi Arabia still derives over 60% of its fiscal revenue from oil. Russia's great power status is similarly highly dependent on energy exports—oil and natural gas have long contributed 30-50% of its federal budget revenue, are the Kremlin's most important single source of income, and are its primary tool for exerting influence over Europe. The dollar's reserve currency status is largely supported by the dollar-denominated pricing mechanism of oil trade. When the strategic value of oil significantly diminishes, all of this must be repriced. For countries whose fiscal revenue depends on oil for over 60%, this is not merely an economic adjustment, but a shattering of the very foundation of their national survival. The Middle East faces not an economic transformation problem, but an existential crisis—the wealth and status accumulated over generations are built on a resource that is depreciating. The same applies to Russia. Without the leverage of energy, Russia's bargaining power in international competition will shrink dramatically. Global trade will also be dismantled from the supply side. Currently, fossil fuels and their derivatives account for a huge proportion of global maritime trade. The essential characteristic of clean energy is localization—sunlight and wind do not need to be transported across oceans, and power plants generate electricity wherever they are built. Energy localization means a significant reduction in the interdependence between countries formed through energy trade. Trade is not just the flow of goods, but also the adhesive between countries. When this adhesive weakens, international relations will become looser and more unpredictable. Thus, two separate lines—AI and energy—converge on the same conclusion. AI eliminates the driving force of global division of labor from the demand side: labor arbitrage no longer makes economic sense. Energy eliminates the necessity of global trade from the supply side: the most important commodities no longer need to be transported across borders. Two completely different variables, with their own independent logical chains, point in the same direction: the form of globalization of the past few decades is coming to an end. Population: The Southward Shift of the World's Center of Gravity Population is the most certain of the three variables. While AI development may encounter technological bottlenecks and energy pathways may experience unexpected breakthroughs or delays, the future of population is almost entirely unpredictable. This is because those who will determine the labor force and population structure two or three decades from now have already been born today—or are already certain they will not be born. Once the fertility rate falls below a certain threshold, it won't recover within decades. This isn't a policy issue, but rather a matter of social inertia. Almost no country has successfully reversed a continuously declining fertility rate. The global population landscape two or three decades from now is largely determined today: the labor force in Europe and East Asia will experience a precipitous decline. According to UN population projections, China's working-age population will decrease from approximately 980 million in 2024 to approximately 750 million in 2050, a reduction of nearly a quarter. Japan and South Korea are entering a period of deep aging. Meanwhile, the young populations of South Asia, Southeast Asia, and Africa will continue to grow. India's working-age population will increase by approximately 140 million during the same period, making it the world's largest labor market. Africa's population will double, with several African countries entering a period of demographic dividend. The global population center of gravity will clearly shift from the north to the south. Population growth itself is a driving force. More people mean more demand, more transactions, more trial and error, and more possibilities. A society with a majority of young people, if provided with basic economic opportunities, becomes a breeding ground for entrepreneurship and risk-taking, and the entire society moves forward; otherwise, the same demographic energy may translate into instability. An aging society is the opposite—no matter how advanced the system, it is permeated with a defensive temperament and a mindset of preservation. Culture certainly has an influence, but generational structure is a more fundamental variable. Over the past two centuries, this driving force has undergone several major shifts. In the 19th century, this shift occurred in Europe; in the first half of the 20th century, it moved to North America; and in the second half, it shifted to East Asia. Each shift was accompanied by a reshaping of the global landscape. In the next two or three decades, this momentum will shift to South Asia and Africa. This is not a prediction, but an extension of an already occurring fact on the timeline. In the AI ​​era, the form of the demographic dividend has fundamentally changed. The past demographic dividend was "cheap labor"—a large number of young people willing to work in factories at low wages, providing a cost advantage for global manufacturing. This dividend is indeed depreciating in the face of AI. However, the new form of the demographic dividend has not disappeared; on the contrary, it has become even more important. Population means a consumer market—as income levels rise, South Asia and Africa will contribute the vast majority of global consumption growth. Population means a talent pool—extreme talent is a probabilistic event; the larger the population base, the greater the absolute number of geniuses at the far right of the distribution. However, whether this potential can be realized still depends on whether basic education and the institutional environment can identify and cultivate geniuses. Population also signifies the fiscal tax base—the total amount of resources a nation can mobilize ultimately depends on how many people are creating and exchanging value. Whoever possesses the largest market has the strongest demand attraction; whoever has the largest talent pool is most likely to generate leading innovations—provided the institutional environment allows talent to emerge. Conversely, the predicament of aging countries is deeper than it appears on the surface. Population decline not only means labor shortages—which can perhaps be partially offset by AI—but also a continued contraction in domestic demand. All economic models of the past few decades have been based on market expansion. After the population declines, it's not a slowdown in growth, but rather the failure of the growth paradigm itself. Japan's experience over the past thirty years has been caused by policy mistakes, but more fundamentally by a struggle against the irreversible physical reality of its population structure. Pension and healthcare expenditures will consume an increasingly larger proportion of public finances, squeezing out investment in education, infrastructure, and defense. National capabilities are locked up precisely when transformation is most needed—not only fiscally, but also psychologically: aging societies tend towards conservatism and rigidity, losing the capacity for risk-taking precisely when they most need to embrace change. Moreover, a more fundamental fact should not be overlooked: South Asia and Africa still have a significant backlog of industrialization and modernization to catch up on. The urbanization of billions of people, infrastructure development, the establishment of manufacturing from scratch, and the improvement of public service systems—historical processes already completed in developed countries—are only just beginning or far from finished in these southern countries. Simply catching up on these lessons is enough to unleash enormous economic growth potential. This growth is not dependent on globalization channels, but rather endogenous and structural. Even without considering changes in political systems, industrialization and modernization alone can bring about profound changes in these regions that will last for decades. However, endogenous industrialization has its limits. To leap to global power, domestic demand alone is insufficient. And precisely at this point, a tremendous tension exists. Population growth is shifting southward, but the channels for receiving this growth are closing. In the past, East Asia successfully transformed its demographic dividend into economic growth through the channels provided by globalization—cheap labor integrated into the global division of labor through export processing, gradually accumulating capital and technology. But this channel is being blocked by multiple forces: AI has eliminated the economic logic of labor arbitrage, energy localization has weakened the cohesion of international trade, and political polarization has closed the door to immigration. Southern countries have the momentum for growth, but may not find an outlet to release it. A historical misalignment has emerged between the driving force and the channel. Looking back at each past shift of growth—from Europe to North America, from North America to East Asia—the receiving countries took decades to truly rise, experiencing war, revolution, and institutional reconstruction. There has never been a smooth transition. This round in South Asia and Africa will likely be no exception. Whether a new way can be found to transform the enormous population energy into national power and international influence is the greatest uncertainty in the population variable, and a key issue determining the future world power landscape. Conclusion These three variables, each irreversible and evolving independently, simultaneously affect the same world. AI eliminates the middle layer, energy eliminates resource dependence, and population reshapes the underlying distribution of national power. Every pillar of the current international order—the globalized trade system, the fossil fuel-dominated geopolitical landscape, the structural advantages of northern countries, and the social stability supported by the middle class—will be redefined. These three variables also share a common direction: they all weaken the power of the "center." AI weakens the advantage of large organizations over small teams, energy localization weakens the control of resource-exporting countries over importing countries, and the collapse of globalization allows power to be withdrawn from the global system to regions and countries. The world order of the past two centuries has been built on the logic of centralization—large factories, large corporations, large countries, and a unified global market. These three variables simultaneously undermine the very logic of centralization itself. There's no need to try to predict the final state. But some things are certain: everything we take for granted today—globalization, middle-class society, the fossil fuel order, the North-dominated world order—is not a steady state on a two- or three-decade timescale. Understanding the direction of each of these three lines is enough to re-examine all our certainties today.</div></div>

data.title: When companies stop buying BTC: Is Strategy the only one struggling to stay afloat?
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Over the past month, a highly unusual phenomenon has emerged in the Bitcoin market.

Compared to the crypto treasury boom of a few months ago, BTC has now moved beyond a state of mass buying.

Buyers are basically limited to Strategy.

I. Only Strategy is still buying

According to CryptoQuant's analysis, Bitcoin treasury demand is currently entirely driven by Strategy. In the past 30 days, Strategy bought 45,000 Bitcoins, while other companies combined bought only about 1,000, a 99% decrease from before, and their share of total purchases dropped to 2%, indicating that new demand has almost completely disappeared. Currently, Strategy holds about 76% of the Bitcoin treasury share, indicating a highly concentrated industry and a lack of broad corporate demand.

Strategy bought BTC 4 times in March, with the most recent purchase on March 23.

...

Currently holding 762,099 BTC, ranking first in holdings and accounting for 3.629% of the total supply of 21 million.

Furthermore, seemingly to continue accumulating BTC, Strategy announced a new $21 billion STRC equity offering and a new $21 billion MSTR equity offering on March 23. The total financing amount reaches a staggering $42 billion.

Strategy's revised ATM equity offering plan allows it to gradually sell more shares to the public market, instead of raising smaller amounts of capital from external investors through convertible bonds as before.

Strategy's revised ATM equity offering plan allows it to gradually sell more shares to the public market, rather than raising smaller amounts of capital from external investors through convertible bonds as before.

Strategy's preferred shares, such as STRC and STRK, pay monthly dividends to investors, while enabling Strategy to increase its Bitcoin holdings without issuing additional MSTR common stock. [Image of Strategy's preferred shares] Corporate buying of BTC has evolved from multiple companies buying together to almost exclusively Strategy buying, with corporate BTC treasury demand almost vanishing instantly. CryptoQuant's Head of Research, Julio Moreno, pointed out at the beginning of the year: "Most new corporate Bitcoin buyers only made one or two purchases before trading stopped, failing to provide sustained price support." Compared to the expansion phase of 2023-2024, year-over-year BTC demand growth has slowed significantly. Currently, this indicator is below the historical trend line, indicating that capital is contracting rather than the adoption wave that usually drives bull markets.

II. Why are other companies not buying?

1. Declining Risk Appetite

The expectation of further interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve is uncertain. With current high interest rates, the cost of capital is high, and stable returns can be obtained through US Treasury bonds. Therefore, crypto asset allocation has become a non-preferred option.

In addition, the global economic downturn and frequent geopolitical conflicts have led most companies to adopt conservative financial strategies.

2. Crypto Market Turns Bearish

In 2025, a large number of cryptocurrency treasury companies emerged, providing Wall Street investors with another way to invest in cryptocurrencies. As Bitcoin continued to climb, reaching its peak in October, the stock prices of many companies also rose. However, the overall decline in the crypto market subsequently impacted the valuations of these companies.

MoreMarkets co-founder and CEO Altan Tutar made a pessimistic prediction about crypto treasury companies last year: "Most Bitcoin treasury companies will disappear like other DATs." After October last year, the crypto market began its downward trend, with new demand slowing and even showing signs of "demand exhaustion." For most companies, crypto treasury reserves are no longer a good option; volatility and the downward trend have directly shaken the confidence of most companies in buying them. Previously, many companies hoarded various cryptocurrencies because it could create a positive cycle. After issuing shares, they would buy a certain coin; holding that coin would attract more market attention, thus driving up the stock price; after raising funds, they would continue to buy… This self-sustaining model requires a consistently bull market, but when the crypto market is in a downturn, this model becomes ineffective. A market downturn leads to a reduction in a company's book profits, which in turn weakens investor confidence, putting pressure on stock prices and further impacting the company's financing capabilities. Ultimately, the company lacks spare cash to buy more… For example, at the end of February, Ethereum treasury FG Nexus reduced its holdings by 7,550 ETH, worth approximately $14.06 million. The reason for the reduction was losses: the company had purchased 50,600 ETH during the DAT company boom in August-September 2025, at an average price of approximately $3,940, for a total value of $200 million. Now, FG Nexus has accumulated losses of $86.98 million due to its ETH investments. When the price of BTC continues to fluctuate downwards, many crypto treasury companies will stop buying cryptocurrencies and instead favor more conservative cash and low-risk asset management. Companies flocked to the market during the bull market and then scattered during the bear market. Stable buyers became cyclical participants. III. Why is Strategy still a seasoned player? While most companies choose to exit the market, Strategy is a notable exception. First, for most companies, BTC and other cryptocurrencies are part of their asset allocation, but for Strategy, BTC is the source of the company's valuation logic, a core asset, and the foundation of its narrative—Strategy has consistently played the role of a BTC bull in both bull and bear markets. Second, for Strategy, BTC has become a belief. Michael Saylor positions Bitcoin as "Digital Capital," calling it the ultimate reserve asset of the 21st century. On March 22, Saylor stated, "The Orange March continues." Despite the market crash last weekend causing a 10% loss on the company's Bitcoin investments, the company still increased its Bitcoin holdings.

Finally, Strategy is deeply tied to BTC. Investors buying Strategy's stock are essentially buying BTC exposure. Strategy has essentially transformed itself into an ETF with a leveraged structure. Therefore, when the market is down, Strategy will continue to increase its holdings.

In summary, even if the overall crypto market is unfavorable, driven by long-term faith, counter-cyclical averaging down, and a continued strengthening of its BTC exposure, Strategy will continue to buy BTC even when the market is declining.

In conclusion, even if the overall crypto market is unfavorable, driven by long-term faith, counter-cyclical averaging down, and a continued strengthening of its BTC exposure, Strategy will continue to buy BTC even when the market is down.

Bernstein analysts believe that "Strategy plays the role of 'Bitcoin's last safe-haven bank,' and Bitcoin ETFs are attracting more resilient (and less speculative) funding sources. Bitcoin's robust capital base is growing." IV. The Impact of Strategy's "Lone Star" In the short term, Strategy's continued buying will have a positive effect on BTC prices. When other companies stop buying, Strategy's stable buying will alleviate market selling pressure and prevent a precipitous drop in BTC prices. However, this support is also affected by Strategy: factors such as lower-than-expected fundraising and slower accumulation will weaken Strategy's ability to accumulate, which may further lead to a decline in BTC prices. In the long term, the increasing concentration of BTC in the hands of a single company is detrimental to the market's resilience. Once Strategy adjusts its strategy, it will further affect confidence in the crypto market. Strategy is no longer just a participant, but a key variable affecting the stability of the crypto market.

data.title: Understanding the US SEC's Token Classification System
data.description:
Author: Jack Inabinet Source: Bankless Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Last week, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) jointly released landmark regulatory guidance. This long-awaited joint interpretation details under what circumstances and in what ways federal securities laws apply to crypto assets.

The crypto industry has long existed in a legal gray area, and this new framework attempts to eliminate some of the uncertainty with a token classification system, helping to define under what conditions digital assets will be considered securities.

Today we'll break down this joint guidance and speculate on how it will ultimately classify mainstream digital assets.

According to the SEC's definition and guidance, "digital goods" refer to crypto assets whose value derives from their functional role in the crypto ecosystem and from market supply and demand. This broad definition covers utility tokens on numerous mature blockchain networks. The SEC has explicitly listed 16 crypto assets that fall into this category, including: Aptos (APT), Avalanche (AVAX), Bitcoin (BTC), Bitcoin Cash (BCH), Cardano (ADA), Chainlink (LINK), Dogecoin (DOGE), Ether (ETH), Hedera (HBAR), Litecoin (LTC), Polkadot (DOT), Shiba Inu (SHIB), Solana (SOL), Stellar (XLM), Tezos (XTZ), and XRP. While the SEC did not explain why each of these cryptocurrencies was classified as a non-security digital commodity, the guidance emphasizes that they do not meet the legal definition of a "security" and therefore will not be automatically considered securities. The SEC states that its use of the term "digital commodity" is strictly based on economics and is used to describe the typical characteristics of a class of non-security digital assets. The guidance also points out that, apart from stablecoins that meet the requirements of the GENIUS Act, any crypto asset that meets the definition of a "commodity" under the Commodity Exchange Act can be considered a true commodity and thus subject to specific regulation by the CFTC. Popular tokens not mentioned by the SEC but potentially classified as "digital goods" also include: BONK: Solana ecosystem meme coin, which has gradually developed governance and various practical functions since its launch. CC: Canton blockchain native utility token used for trading, settlement, data synchronization, and asset transfer. EIGEN: EigenLayer ecosystem restaking utility token used to provide economic security. ETC: Ethereum's proof-of-work version, already recognized by the SEC as a non-security digital commodity. HYPE: Hyperliquid network native gas token. STX: Stacks Bitcoin Layer 2 network native gas and staking reward token. SUI: Sui network native gas and staking token. TAO: Bittensor network native token used to incentivize machine learning contributions in the decentralized AI market. TRX: Tron network native gas and staking token.
